enrichment.short_description: Rio SEO is an enterprise local marketing platform that helps multi-location brands manage local listings, reviews, store locators, and local search visibility across search engines, maps, and directories.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom quote-based pricing for enterprise accounts; no public price list
enrichment.seo_title: Rio SEO Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Local SEO Platform Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rio SEO is an enterprise local experience platform for listings, reviews, and store locators. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rio SEO is an enterprise-grade local marketing and search platform, often described as a Local Experience (LX) platform, built for large multi-location brands such as retailers, restaurant chains, healthcare systems, and financial services companies that need to manage online presence across hundreds or thousands of physical locations. Founded in 2006 and headquartered in San Diego, California, Rio SEO provides a suite of tools covering local listings management (syncing business information such as name, address, phone, and hours across Google Business Profile, Apple Maps, Bing, Yelp, Facebook, Nextdoor, and other directories and data aggregators), review and reputation management, local SEO content and landing page generation, store locator software, and local search analytics and reporting. The platform is aimed squarely at enterprise marketing teams and franchise or multi-location businesses rather than solo marketers or small local businesses, and its customer base has included well-known brands like Claire's, Wyndham Destinations, and European Wax Center. Rio SEO does not publish self-serve pricing; instead, prospective customers work with a sales team to get a custom quote based on the number of locations, modules needed, and service level, which typically includes account management and support as part of enterprise contracts. In July 2022, Rio SEO merged with Forsta (a customer experience and market research technology company), which combined Rio SEO's local marketing capabilities with Forsta's broader customer experience and voice-of-customer platform, though Rio SEO continues to operate and market its Local Experience platform under its own brand. Because it targets enterprise buyers, Rio SEO competes most directly with other multi-location marketing platforms such as Yext, Uberall, and SOCi rather than with general-purpose SEO tools like Ahrefs or SEMrush, and its value proposition centers on consistency and accuracy of local business data at scale, review volume and sentiment management, and driving foot traffic and online conversions for brick-and-mortar brands. Marketing teams evaluating Rio SEO should expect an implementation and onboarding process typical of enterprise martech, with dedicated support rather than instant self-service signup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rio SEO positions itself as a Local Experience (LX) platform for enterprise brands, combining local listings management, reputation management, local SEO, and store locator technology into a single suite aimed at organizations with many physical locations. Founded in 2006 and based in San Diego, California, the company has spent nearly two decades focused specifically on the local search needs of large, multi-location businesses in retail, restaurant, hospitality, healthcare, and financial services verticals.

In 2022 Rio SEO merged with Forsta, a customer experience and research technology company, expanding the combined organization's footprint in experience management while Rio SEO's local marketing product line continued to operate under its own name for local search and listings use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's core capability is local listings management, which keeps business name, address, phone number, hours, and other attributes synchronized and accurate across Google Business Profile, Apple Maps, Bing Places, Yelp, Facebook, Nextdoor, and dozens of other directories and data aggregators that feed local search results. This is paired with review and reputation management tools that let brands monitor, respond to, and analyze customer reviews across locations from a centralized dashboard.

Beyond listings and reviews, Rio SEO offers store locator software that brands can embed on their websites to help customers find nearby locations, local landing page and content generation to support local SEO at scale, and reporting and analytics that roll location-level performance up into brand-wide dashboards for marketing teams to track visibility, engagement, and reputation trends.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rio SEO does not publish self-serve pricing tiers on its website. As an enterprise platform, pricing is quote-based and depends on factors such as the number of locations being managed, which product modules (listings, reviews, store locator, etc.) are included, and the level of managed services or support required. Prospective customers request a demo or consultation with the Rio SEO sales team to receive a custom proposal.

features.description: Generates and manages local SEO landing pages at scale across hundreds or thousands of locations.

features.title: Local search analytics and reporting
features.description: Rolls up location-level visibility, engagement, and reputation data into brand-wide dashboards for marketing teams.

features.title: Online ordering integrations
features.description: Supports online ordering and local commerce integrations for restaurant and retail brands.

features.description: Enterprise contracts typically include dedicated account management and onboarding support.

features.description: Built specifically to handle brands with hundreds to thousands of physical locations rather than single-location businesses.

Broad coverage of directories and data aggregators for listings syncing
Combines listings, reviews, and store locator tools in one suite
Nearly two decades of experience in local search for enterprise retail and hospitality brands
Backed by the broader Forsta customer experience organization since the 2022 merger
cons: No public pricing, making it hard to budget or compare without a sales conversation
Overkill and cost-prohibitive for small businesses or single-location companies

faqs.answer: Rio SEO does not publish pricing publicly. Pricing is custom and quote-based, depending on the number of locations and modules required, and is provided after a consultation with their sales team.

faqs.answer: Rio SEO merged with Forsta, a customer experience and market research technology company, in July 2022, though the Rio SEO local marketing product continues to operate under its own brand.

faqs.answer: No. Rio SEO is built for enterprise brands managing many locations; it is not designed or priced for single-location small businesses.

faqs.question: What directories does Rio SEO sync listings to
faqs.answer: Rio SEO syncs local business listings to major platforms including Google Business Profile, Apple Maps, Bing Places, Yelp, Facebook, and Nextdoor, among other directories and data aggregators.

faqs.question: Where is Rio SEO headquartered
faqs.answer: Rio SEO is headquartered in San Diego, California.

faqs.question: When was Rio SEO founded
faqs.answer: Rio SEO was founded in 2006.

faqs.question: What industries use Rio SEO
faqs.answer: Rio SEO's customers span retail, restaurants, hospitality, healthcare, and financial services, with brands like Claire's, Wyndham Destinations, and European Wax Center among its clients.

enrichment.short_description: Rippling is an all-in-one workforce management platform unifying HR, payroll, IT device management, and finance tools on a single employee record for global companies.
enrichment.pricing_model: Modular per-employee-per-month pricing plus a monthly base platform fee; custom quotes
enrichment.starting_price: From $8 per employee per month (plus a base platform fee), with total cost varying by modules
languages: English, with support for multi-country payroll and localization in dozens of countries
enrichment.seo_title: Rippling Review 2026: Pricing, Features, HR, IT and Payroll Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rippling unifies HR, payroll, IT, and finance in one platform. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons, and how the modular pricing model works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rippling is a workforce management platform that unifies HR, payroll, benefits administration, IT device and app management, and finance tools around a single source-of-truth employee record, so that when an employee is hired, promoted, or terminated, changes automatically propagate across every connected system. The company was founded in April 2016 by Parker Conrad and Prasanna Sankar, with Conrad also known as a co-founder of Zenefits; Rippling is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with additional offices including New York, Chicago, and Pune, India. As of 2025, Rippling reported serving more than 20,000 customers worldwide and employing more than 4,000 people across offices on four continents, and the company has been privately valued at roughly $16.8 billion as of a May 2025 funding round. Rippling's product suite spans core HR (employee records, onboarding, org charts), US and international payroll, benefits administration, time and attendance, PEO services, IT management (automated laptop provisioning, app and SaaS management, mobile device management), and finance tools such as corporate cards, expense management, and bill pay, all built on Rippling's own compliance and employment infrastructure across many countries. Pricing is modular and not fully self-serve: the base Rippling Unity platform starts around $8 per employee per month plus a mandatory monthly base fee (commonly cited around $35), and each additional module a company activates, such as payroll, benefits, or IT device management, carries its own separate per-employee-per-month charge, meaning effective total costs commonly range from roughly $20 to $35+ per employee per month depending on which modules are selected; exact enterprise pricing requires a custom quote from Rippling's sales team, and third-party contract-data aggregators have reported median annual Rippling contract values in the range of roughly $39,700 to $40,100 based on real purchase records. Rippling competes most directly with Gusto, Justworks, Deel, and BambooHR, but differentiates itself by combining HR, IT, and finance in one unified system rather than offering HR alone, which appeals particularly to fast-growing, distributed, or global companies that want to reduce the number of disconnected point solutions and manual data syncing between systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rippling is a workforce management platform built around the idea of a single employee record that powers HR, payroll, benefits, IT device management, and finance tools simultaneously, so changes like a new hire or a department transfer automatically ripple across every connected system rather than requiring manual updates in separate tools. Founded in 2016 by Parker Conrad and Prasanna Sankar and headquartered in San Francisco, Rippling has grown into one of the largest HR technology companies, reporting more than 20,000 customers and over 4,000 employees as of 2025.

The platform is designed to scale from small startups to large global enterprises, offering US and international payroll, PEO services, and compliance infrastructure across dozens of countries, which lets companies manage a distributed or global workforce from one system rather than stitching together country-specific vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rippling's core modules include HR management (employee records, onboarding, org charts, and workflows), payroll (domestic and international), benefits administration, time and attendance tracking, and a PEO offering for companies that want Rippling to act as the employer of record for compliance purposes. On the IT side, Rippling automates laptop and hardware provisioning and deprovisioning, manages SaaS app access and licenses, and provides mobile device management, all tied to the same employee record used for HR.

The finance suite adds corporate spend cards, expense management, and bill pay, giving finance teams visibility into spend alongside headcount data. Because every module reads from the same underlying employee record, actions like offboarding an employee can simultaneously revoke app access, stop payroll, and reclaim company hardware in one workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rippling uses a modular, per-employee-per-month pricing model rather than flat plan tiers. The base Unity platform starts at roughly $8 per employee per month plus a separate monthly base platform fee (commonly cited around $35), and each additional module, such as payroll, benefits administration, or IT device management, is priced separately on top of that base, typically pushing effective costs to roughly $20 to $35 or more per employee per month depending on which modules a company activates.

Because pricing is customized to each company's size, module selection, and country footprint, Rippling does not offer fully transparent self-serve pricing; prospective customers need to request a quote through Rippling's sales team to get an exact cost for their configuration.

features.description: Tracks employee hours, overtime, and time-off requests integrated with payroll processing.

features.description: Manages employee benefits enrollment and administration alongside payroll and HR records.

features.title: Spend management and corporate cards
features.description: Provides corporate cards, expense management, and bill pay tools tied to the same employee and org-chart data.

features.title: Workflow automation
features.description: Lets admins build automated workflows triggered by HR events, such as onboarding checklists or offboarding access revocation.

pros: Combines HR, payroll, IT, and finance in one connected platform, reducing manual data syncing
Strong automation for onboarding and offboarding across systems
Supports global payroll and compliance across many countries
Highly modular, letting companies pay only for the functionality they need
Well-funded, well-established vendor with strong customer growth
cons: Pricing is not fully transparent and requires a custom sales quote
Modular add-on pricing can make total cost higher than advertised base price suggests
Can be more complex to configure than single-purpose HR tools for very small teams
Some users report a learning curve given the breadth of the platform

faqs.question: How much does Rippling cost
faqs.answer: Rippling's base platform starts around $8 per employee per month plus a monthly base fee, with additional modules like payroll, benefits, and IT management priced separately, commonly bringing effective costs to roughly $20 to $35 or more per employee per month depending on configuration.

faqs.question: Who founded Rippling
faqs.answer: Rippling was founded in 2016 by Parker Conrad and Prasanna Sankar.

faqs.question: Where is Rippling headquartered
faqs.answer: Rippling is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with additional offices including New York, Chicago, and Pune, India.

faqs.question: What does Rippling do
faqs.answer: Rippling unifies HR, payroll, benefits, IT device and app management, and finance tools around a single employee record so changes propagate automatically across connected systems.

faqs.question: How many employees does Rippling have
faqs.answer: Rippling employed more than 4,000 people as of 2025, serving over 20,000 customers worldwide.

faqs.question: Does Rippling support international payroll
faqs.answer: Yes, Rippling supports payroll and compliance for employees and contractors across dozens of countries.

faqs.question: What is Rippling's valuation
faqs.answer: Rippling was privately valued at approximately $16.8 billion as of a May 2025 funding round.

faqs.question: Who are Rippling's main competitors
faqs.answer: Rippling's main competitors include Gusto, Justworks, Deel, BambooHR, and other HR and payroll platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Rival IQ is a social media analytics and competitive benchmarking platform that helps marketers track their own social performance against competitors and industry peers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered monthly or annual subscription, priced by number of tracked companies and users
enrichment.seo_title: Rival IQ Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Social Media Analytics Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rival IQ is a social media analytics and competitive benchmarking tool. Compare 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rival IQ is a social media analytics and competitive intelligence platform designed for digital marketers who need to benchmark their brand's social performance against competitors and industry peers across networks like Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, X, LinkedIn, and YouTube. The company was founded in 2012 by T.A. McCann, Seth Pollack, and John Clark, and is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, with a small team historically in the range of roughly 10 to 20 employees. Rival IQ was later acquired by NetBase Quid, a consumer and social intelligence company, in 2021, though it continues to operate and be sold as a distinct product. The platform's core value is letting marketing teams create custom 'landscapes' of competitors to track post-level engagement, content performance, hashtag usage, follower growth, and posting cadence side by side, alongside social listening and sentiment tools, Facebook Ads reporting, and Google Data Studio (Looker Studio) connectivity for custom reporting. Rival IQ uses transparent, publicly listed tiered pricing: the entry-level Drive plan is $239 per month (with roughly a 15 percent discount for annual billing), including tracking of 10 companies, 6 months of historical data, and a single user account; the mid-tier Engage plan is $349 per month with 20 tracked companies, 12 months of data history, 2 user accounts, private data analysis, Facebook Ads reporting, and Google Data Studio connectivity; and the top self-serve tier, Engage Pro, is $559 per month with 40 tracked companies, 24 months of history, 5 users, multi-handle aggregation, estimated impressions, and API access. Additional tracked companies and user seats can be purchased a la carte, and larger organizations can request custom enterprise or volume pricing. All plans include core features like social benchmarking, post analysis, unlimited competitive landscapes, hashtag analysis, scheduled exports, and email alerts, and prospective customers can try the platform with a 14-day free trial that requires no credit card. Rival IQ is generally positioned as a mid-market tool for marketing teams, agencies, and social media managers who want deeper competitive benchmarking than native platform analytics provide, competing with tools like Sprout Social, Socialbakers (Emplifi), and Brandwatch, though it is more narrowly focused on competitive social analytics rather than full social media management or publishing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rival IQ is a social media analytics platform built specifically around competitive benchmarking, letting marketers compare their brand's social performance directly against competitors and industry peers rather than only viewing their own account metrics in isolation. Founded in 2012 by T.A. McCann, Seth Pollack, and John Clark, the company is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, and was acquired by NetBase Quid in 2021, continuing to operate as a distinct social analytics product.

The platform is aimed at marketing teams, social media managers, and agencies that need to justify social strategy with data, track share-of-voice against named competitors, and produce custom reporting for stakeholders using tools like Google Data Studio (Looker Studio) integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rival IQ's central feature is the 'landscape,' a custom grouping of competitor and peer social accounts that users can benchmark against, tracking engagement rate, posting frequency, follower growth, and content type performance across Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, X, LinkedIn, and YouTube. The platform also offers social listening with saved searches, hashtag analysis, and post-level content analysis to identify top-performing content themes.

Higher tiers add Facebook Ads reporting, private data analysis for the user's own connected accounts, multi-handle aggregation for brands with several regional or product accounts, estimated impressions, and API access for pulling data into other systems, alongside scheduled report exports and email alerts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rival IQ publishes transparent tiered pricing on its website. The Drive plan starts at $239 per month for 10 tracked companies, 6 months of data history, and 1 user account. The Engage plan is $349 per month for 20 tracked companies, 12 months of history, and 2 users, while Engage Pro is $559 per month for 40 tracked companies, 24 months of history, 5 users, and API access.

All plans offer roughly a 15 percent discount when billed annually, and additional tracked companies or user seats can be added for extra monthly fees. Larger organizations can contact sales for custom enterprise or volume pricing, and a 14-day free trial is available without a credit card.

features.title: Facebook Ads reporting
features.description: Available on Engage and higher plans, reporting on paid social performance alongside organic metrics.

features.description: Automated report exports and email alerts keep stakeholders updated without manual pulls.

features.description: Available on the Engage Pro plan, allowing programmatic access to Rival IQ data for custom integrations.

Google Data Studio integration for custom reporting
cons: Entry-level pricing ($239/month) is high for very small businesses or solo marketers
Data history is capped by plan tier (6 to 24 months)
Not a publishing or scheduling tool, so it must be paired with a separate social management platform
Additional companies and users cost extra beyond plan limits

faqs.question: What does Rival IQ do
faqs.answer: Rival IQ is a social media analytics platform that benchmarks a brand's social performance against competitors, tracking engagement, content performance, hashtags, and growth across major social networks.

faqs.question: How much does Rival IQ cost
faqs.answer: Rival IQ starts at $239 per month for the Drive plan, with Engage at $349 per month and Engage Pro at $559 per month, each billed monthly or with roughly 15 percent off annually.

faqs.question: Who owns Rival IQ
faqs.answer: Rival IQ was acquired by NetBase Quid, a consumer and social intelligence company, in 2021.

faqs.answer: Yes, Rival IQ offers a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: Does Rival IQ support TikTok analytics
faqs.answer: Yes, Rival IQ tracks performance across Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, X, LinkedIn, and YouTube.

faqs.question: Where is Rival IQ based
faqs.answer: Rival IQ is headquartered in Seattle, Washington.

faqs.question: When was Rival IQ founded
faqs.answer: Rival IQ was founded in 2012 by T.A. McCann, Seth Pollack, and John Clark.

faqs.question: Can Rival IQ publish or schedule social posts
faqs.answer: No, Rival IQ is an analytics and benchmarking tool, not a publishing or scheduling platform.

enrichment.short_description: Rive is a real-time interactive design and animation tool that lets designers and developers create animated, state-driven graphics for apps, games, and websites.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with per-seat monthly subscription tiers plus a custom enterprise plan
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start at $9/seat/month (Cadet)
enrichment.seo_title: Rive Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Interactive Animation Tool Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rive is a real-time interactive animation and design tool for apps, games, and web. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rive is a real-time interactive design and animation tool that lets designers and developers create vector-based animations with built-in interactivity, state machines, and lightweight runtimes that render natively on web, iOS, Android, game engines, and other platforms without needing to export video files. The company behind Rive was founded in 2016 in San Francisco, California, originally under the name 2Dimensions, and built early tools including Nima and Flare before consolidating its product and company brand into Rive in December 2019 under co-founders and identical twin brothers Guido Rosso (CEO) and Luigi Rosso (CTO). Rive has raised approximately $14 million in funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Two Sigma Ventures, Unusual Ventures, and BMW i Ventures, and employed roughly 75 people as of 2026. The core differentiator of Rive versus traditional animation tools like After Effects or Lottie is its State Machine feature, which lets designers define interactive states and transitions (such as a button's hover, press, and loading states) directly inside the design tool, then ship that interactivity to production via small, fast runtimes rather than requiring a developer to hand-code the logic separately; this makes Rive popular for animated app icons, onboarding flows, game characters, and interactive UI elements where file size and real-time responsiveness matter, and notable users include companies like Duolingo. Rive's pricing is freemium and per-seat: the Free plan supports 3 collaborative files with limited AI agent access; the Cadet plan is $9 per seat per month (up to 3 seats) and unlocks unlimited files and .riv file export; the Voyager plan is $32 per seat per month (up to 25 seats) and adds shared libraries, embeddable URL hosting, and more advanced AI agent credits; and the Enterprise plan is $120 per seat per month, intended for organizations with $10 million or more in annual revenue, and includes subteam workspaces, custom S3 storage, SSO, SOC 2 Type II compliance, dedicated support, and centralized billing. Rive's collaborative editor supports real-time multiplayer editing similar to Figma, and its open runtime libraries are widely used by mobile and game developers who need lightweight, interactive vector graphics rather than static image or video assets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rive is a real-time interactive animation and design tool used by designers and developers to build vector-based, state-driven animations that run natively inside apps, games, and websites. The company was founded in 2016 in San Francisco under the name 2Dimensions, building early 2D animation tools before rebranding the company and its flagship product to Rive in December 2019 under twin co-founders Guido Rosso and Luigi Rosso.

Rive has raised roughly $14 million from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Two Sigma Ventures, Unusual Ventures, and BMW i Ventures, and had grown to approximately 75 employees as of 2026, with notable adopters including Duolingo for in-app interactive animations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rive's signature feature is its State Machine, which lets designers build interactive logic (hover states, button presses, loading animations, character behaviors) directly in the visual editor, then export that interactivity as a compact .riv file that developers integrate using Rive's lightweight open-source runtimes for web, iOS, Android, Flutter, React Native, Unity, and other platforms.

The collaborative editor supports real-time multiplayer editing similar to Figma, and higher tiers add shared component libraries across files, embeddable URL hosting for quick previews or lightweight production use, and AI-assisted authoring features with monthly agent credits.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rive offers a Free plan with 3 collaborative files and limited AI agent access. The Cadet plan costs $9 per seat per month for up to 3 seats and unlocks unlimited files and export of .riv files. The Voyager plan is $32 per seat per month for up to 25 seats, adding shared libraries and embeddable hosting.

The Enterprise plan is $120 per seat per month and is intended for companies with at least $10 million in annual revenue, adding SSO, SOC 2 Type II compliance, dedicated support, custom runtimes, and centralized billing.

features.title: State Machine interactivity
features.description: Visually define interactive states and transitions (hover, press, load) without writing custom animation logic by hand.

features.title: Real-time collaborative editor
features.description: Multiplayer editing similar to Figma, letting design and engineering teams work in the same file simultaneously.

features.title: Lightweight cross-platform runtimes
features.description: Open-source runtimes for web, iOS, Android, Flutter, React Native, and Unity render .riv files natively and efficiently.

features.title: Vector-based animation format
features.description: Produces small, scalable .riv files instead of bulky video or sprite-sheet exports.

features.title: Shared component libraries
features.description: Available on Voyager and higher, letting teams reuse animated components across multiple files.

features.title: Embeddable URL hosting
features.description: Lets teams host and embed Rive animations directly via URL for quick previews or lightweight production use.

features.title: AI-assisted authoring
features.description: Built-in AI agent features with monthly credits to help accelerate animation creation, expanded on paid tiers.

features.title: Enterprise security and compliance
features.description: SOC 2 Type II compliance, SSO, and custom S3 storage available on the Enterprise plan.

pros: Interactive State Machine lets designers build logic without developer hand-coding
Produces very small, performant files compared to video-based animation exports
Real-time collaborative editing similar to Figma
Open-source runtimes across many platforms including web, mobile, and game engines
Free plan available for learning and small projects
cons: Enterprise plan requires $10M+ annual revenue and is priced significantly higher than lower tiers
Learning curve for designers unfamiliar with state-machine-based animation logic
Seat limits on Cadet (3) and Voyager (25) plans may require jumping straight to Enterprise for larger teams
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to long-established tools like After Effects

faqs.question: What is Rive used for
faqs.answer: Rive is used to design real-time interactive animations, such as animated app icons, onboarding screens, game characters, and interactive UI elements, that run natively on web, mobile, and game platforms.

faqs.question: How much does Rive cost
faqs.answer: Rive has a free plan, with paid tiers starting at $9 per seat per month (Cadet), $32 per seat per month (Voyager), and $120 per seat per month for Enterprise.

faqs.question: Who founded Rive
faqs.answer: Rive was founded in 2016 in San Francisco by twin brothers Guido Rosso and Luigi Rosso, originally under the name 2Dimensions before rebranding to Rive in December 2019.

faqs.question: What makes Rive different from After Effects or Lottie
faqs.answer: Rive lets designers build interactive state-based logic directly in the editor and ships it via small, fast runtimes, rather than exporting static video or JSON animation files without built-in interactivity.

faqs.question: Does Rive support Flutter and React Native
faqs.answer: Yes, Rive provides open-source runtimes for Flutter, React Native, iOS, Android, web, and Unity, among other platforms.

faqs.question: Who uses Rive
faqs.answer: Rive is used by design and engineering teams at companies including Duolingo for interactive in-app animations.

faqs.question: Is Rive open source
faqs.answer: Rive's runtimes and the .riv file format are open source, while the collaborative editor itself is a commercial, subscription-based product.

faqs.question: Where is Rive headquartered
faqs.answer: Rive is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

use_cases.title: Interactive app onboarding
use_cases.description: A mobile app team designs animated onboarding screens with state-driven transitions that respond to user taps without custom-coded animation logic.
use_cases.audience: Mobile app designers and front-end developers

use_cases.title: Game character animation
use_cases.description: A game studio builds interactive character animations with state machines that react to gameplay events in real time.
use_cases.audience: Game designers and developers

use_cases.title: Animated product UI elements
use_cases.description: A product team creates lightweight animated icons and micro-interactions that ship as small .riv files instead of video assets, improving app performance.
use_cases.audience: Product design teams and front-end engineers

enrichment.short_description: Riverside is a remote recording and production platform for podcasts and video, offering local high-quality multi-track recording, AI editing, and publishing tools.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with tiered monthly or annual subscriptions plus custom Business pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start at $24/month (Pro, billed annually)
enrichment.headquarters: Tel Aviv, Israel (with additional offices in New York and Amsterdam)
enrichment.seo_title: Riverside Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Podcast Recording Platform Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Riverside (formerly Riverside.fm) is a remote podcast and video recording platform. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Riverside, formerly branded Riverside.fm and rebranded to Riverside.com, is a remote recording and content production platform used by podcasters, video creators, marketers, and businesses to record high-quality, multi-track audio and video with remote guests over the internet. The company was founded in 2020 by brothers Nadav Keyson (CEO) and Gideon Keyson (CTO), who grew up in Amsterdam before relocating to Israel, and launched the product just as the COVID-19 pandemic drove a surge in remote content creation; the company is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with additional offices in New York and Amsterdam. Riverside has raised a total of roughly $77 million across funding rounds, including a $35 million Series B in April 2022 led by Zeev Ventures, and has grown from a small team into a company with employees in the range of roughly 90 to 150 people. The platform's core technical advantage is that it records each participant's audio and video locally on their own device at up to 4K video and 48kHz audio, then uploads the files afterward, which avoids the compression artifacts and dropped frames typical of live video-call recording, and it has expanded well beyond recording into a broader production suite including AI-powered editing (text-based editing, Magic Clips for auto-generating short clips), a teleprompter, live streaming and webinar hosting, podcast and video hosting with analytics, and direct publishing to platforms like YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, Instagram, and LinkedIn. Pricing is freemium: the Free plan includes 2 hours of one-off multi-track recording at up to 720p with a Riverside watermark; the Pro plan is $24/month billed annually (or $29/month billed monthly) and includes 1 studio, 15 hours of separate track downloads, 4K video, no watermark, and the AI editing agent; the Grow plan is $34/month annually ($39 monthly) and adds social scheduling, a creator website, and multistreaming; the Webinar plan is $79/month annually ($99 monthly) and adds webinar hosting for up to 100 registrants; and the Business plan offers custom pricing with unlimited studios, unlimited track downloads, SSO, SOC 2 and ISO 27001 compliance, and a dedicated customer success manager. Riverside is commonly compared to tools like SquadCast, Zencastr, and StreamYard, and it is distinct from the animation tool Rive (rive.app), despite the similar-sounding name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Riverside is a remote recording and content production platform originally launched as Riverside.fm in 2020 by brothers Nadav Keyson (CEO) and Gideon Keyson (CTO), who are natively from Amsterdam and relocated to Israel; the company is headquartered in Tel Aviv, with additional offices in New York and Amsterdam. Riverside has since rebranded to Riverside.com and raised approximately $77 million in total funding, including a $35 million Series B round in April 2022 led by Zeev Ventures.

The platform is used primarily by podcasters, video creators, marketing teams, and businesses running remote interviews, webinars, or branded video content, and it has grown well beyond simple recording into a broader production and publishing suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Riverside's core technology records each participant locally on their own device at up to 4K video and 48kHz audio, then uploads the separate tracks after the session, avoiding the quality loss and connectivity issues common with live video-call recording. This local-recording approach is paired with AI-powered editing tools, including text-based editing and 'Magic Clips' that automatically generate short social clips from longer recordings, plus a built-in teleprompter for hosts.

Beyond recording and editing, Riverside offers podcast and video hosting with analytics, direct publishing to platforms like YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, Instagram, and LinkedIn, live streaming and multistreaming to multiple destinations at once, and webinar hosting with registration forms, lead capture, polls, and Q&A for higher-tier plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Riverside offers a Free plan with 2 hours of one-off multi-track recording at up to 720p video with a watermark. The Pro plan is $24/month billed annually ($29/month billed monthly) and includes 1 studio, 15 hours of separate track downloads, 4K video, no watermark, and AI editing tools.

The Grow plan is $34/month annually ($39 monthly) with 2 studios and social scheduling; the Webinar plan is $79/month annually ($99 monthly) with webinar hosting for up to 100 registrants; and the Business plan offers custom pricing with unlimited studios, SSO, and compliance certifications for larger organizations.

features.title: Local multi-track recording
features.description: Records each participant locally at up to 4K video and 48kHz audio, uploading afterward to avoid live-call quality loss.

features.title: AI editing and Magic Clips
features.description: Text-based editing and AI-generated short clips for repurposing long-form recordings into social content.

features.title: Podcast and video hosting
features.description: Built-in hosting with analytics and direct publishing to YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, Instagram, and LinkedIn.

features.title: Live streaming and multistreaming
features.description: Stream live in up to Full HD to multiple destinations simultaneously from Grow plan and above.

features.title: Webinar hosting
features.description: Host webinars for up to 100 registrants (10,000 on Business) with registration forms, lead capture, polls, and Q&A.

features.title: Teleprompter
features.description: Built-in teleprompter for hosts, extended to guests on the Business plan.

features.title: Producer mode and collaborative editing
features.description: Business plan features for managing multi-person production teams with custom roles and collaborative editing.

features.title: Enterprise integrations and compliance
features.description: Business plan adds Salesforce, Marketo, and HubSpot integrations, API access, SSO, and SOC 2/ISO 27001 compliance.

pros: Local recording avoids the audio/video quality loss common in live video-call tools
Broad all-in-one suite covering recording, editing, hosting, and publishing
AI-powered clip generation speeds up social content repurposing
Generous free plan for trying the platform before subscribing
Well-funded company with strong track record of feature releases
cons: Free plan is limited to 2 one-off hours of multi-track recording
Higher-tier plans (Grow, Webinar, Business) add up quickly for teams needing full features
Business plan pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation
Some advanced production features are locked behind the top-tier Business plan

faqs.question: What is Riverside used for
faqs.answer: Riverside is used to record remote podcasts, video interviews, and webinars in high quality, then edit and publish that content using built-in AI editing and distribution tools.

faqs.question: How much does Riverside cost
faqs.answer: Riverside has a free plan, with paid plans starting at $24/month (Pro, billed annually), up to $79/month (Webinar), and custom pricing for the Business plan.

faqs.question: Who founded Riverside
faqs.answer: Riverside was founded in 2020 by brothers Nadav Keyson and Gideon Keyson.

faqs.question: Is Riverside the same as Rive
faqs.answer: No. Riverside (riverside.fm / riverside.com) is a podcast and video recording platform, while Rive (rive.app) is an unrelated real-time interactive animation and design tool.

faqs.question: Why does Riverside record locally instead of over a video call
faqs.answer: Recording locally on each participant's device avoids the compression, dropped frames, and audio glitches that can occur when recording directly from a live video call, resulting in higher-quality source files.

faqs.question: Where is Riverside headquartered
faqs.answer: Riverside is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with additional offices in New York and Amsterdam.

faqs.question: Does Riverside offer live streaming
faqs.answer: Yes, Riverside supports live streaming and multistreaming to multiple destinations starting on the Grow plan.

faqs.question: How much funding has Riverside raised
faqs.answer: Riverside has raised approximately $77 million in total funding, including a $35 million Series B round in April 2022.

enrichment.short_description: Roadie is a hosted, managed internal developer portal built on the open-source Backstage framework, giving engineering teams a software catalog without self-hosting Backstage.
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-developer-per-month subscription plus custom enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: From $24 per developer per month (Teams plan)
enrichment.seo_title: Roadie Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Backstage Developer Portal Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Roadie is a hosted Backstage-based internal developer portal. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons, and how it compares to self-hosting Backstage.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Roadie is a fully hosted and managed internal developer portal built on top of Spotify's open-source Backstage framework, letting engineering organizations get a software catalog, technical documentation hub, and developer self-service platform without the overhead of running and maintaining Backstage themselves. The company was founded in 2020, timed to start offering Backstage as a hosted service as soon as the open-source framework was released, and operates as a fully remote, headquartered-in-Dublin, Ireland company with a small team generally estimated in the range of roughly 20 to 30 employees. Roadie describes itself as the second-largest contributor to the Backstage project after Spotify itself, and its customers include companies such as Netlify, Snyk, Contentful, and MyFitnessPal. The core product provides a Backstage-compatible software catalog (tracking services, APIs, and ownership across an engineering organization), TechDocs and API specification hosting, self-service automation templates ('software templates' in Backstage terminology) for scaffolding new services, access to more than 75 open-source Backstage plugins, single sign-on, and more recently RAG-based AI search and MCP (Model Context Protocol) server access for connecting AI coding agents to the organization's software catalog context. Roadie also sells a separate 'Enterprise Context' product, an integration-powered data layer that pulls together data from many pre-built and custom sources into a permissioned context bundle that can be used standalone or bundled with the developer portal, aimed at powering AI agents with reliable organizational context. Pricing is per 'contributing developer' (engineers who write code tracked in the catalog; non-coding users can access it for free), with the Teams plan priced at $24 per developer per month for organizations of roughly 50 to 150 developers with unlimited catalog entities, while the Growth plan and Enterprise Context product use custom pricing that adds SLAs, custom private Backstage plugins, REST API access, advanced search, secure on-premise connections, usage analytics dashboards, and custom role-based access control for larger organizations. Roadie has raised roughly $3.7 million in funding from investors including Boldstart Ventures and Firstminute Capital, and it is positioned against alternatives like self-hosting Backstage in-house, Spotify's own commercial Backstage offering (Portal), Cortex, and OpsLevel in the internal developer platform category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Roadie is a hosted and managed internal developer portal built on Spotify's open-source Backstage framework, letting engineering teams adopt a Backstage-style software catalog and developer portal without the operational burden of deploying, upgrading, and maintaining Backstage themselves. Founded in 2020 and headquartered in Dublin, Ireland, as a fully remote company, Roadie describes itself as the second-largest contributor to the Backstage open-source project after Spotify.

The company serves engineering organizations such as Netlify, Snyk, Contentful, and MyFitnessPal, and has raised approximately $3.7 million from investors including Boldstart Ventures and Firstminute Capital to grow its hosted Backstage and, more recently, AI context offerings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Roadie's core product is a Backstage-compatible software catalog that tracks services, APIs, and ownership across an engineering organization, paired with TechDocs and API specification hosting, self-service scaffolding templates for creating new services, and access to more than 75 open-source Backstage plugins, all delivered as a managed SaaS with automatic upgrades rather than self-hosted infrastructure.

More recently, Roadie has added AI-oriented capabilities including RAG-based search and MCP (Model Context Protocol) server access, letting AI coding agents query the organization's software catalog for context, and a separate Enterprise Context product that aggregates data from many internal and external sources into permissioned context bundles for AI agents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Roadie prices its developer portal per 'contributing developer' (engineers whose code is tracked in the catalog), with non-coding users able to log in for free. The Teams plan is $24 per developer per month for organizations of roughly 50 to 150 developers with unlimited catalog entities.

The Growth plan, for 100 or more developers, and the standalone or bundled Enterprise Context product both use custom pricing, adding features like SLAs, custom private plugins, REST API access, secure on-premise connections, and custom role-based access control.

Smaller company (roughly 20-30 employees) compared to larger enterprise software vendors

faqs.question: What is Roadie used for
faqs.answer: Roadie is used as a hosted, managed internal developer portal built on Backstage, giving engineering teams a software catalog, documentation hub, and self-service tooling without self-hosting Backstage.

faqs.question: How much does Roadie cost
faqs.answer: Roadie's Teams plan starts at $24 per developer per month for organizations of roughly 50 to 150 developers, with Growth and Enterprise Context pricing available on request for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Is Roadie the same as Backstage
faqs.answer: No. Backstage is Spotify's open-source developer portal framework; Roadie is a commercial, fully hosted and managed version of Backstage that removes the need to self-host it.

faqs.question: When was Roadie founded
faqs.answer: Roadie was founded in 2020, timed to launch as Backstage was released as an open-source project.

faqs.question: Where is Roadie headquartered
faqs.answer: Roadie is headquartered in Dublin, Ireland, and operates as a fully remote company.

faqs.question: What companies use Roadie
faqs.answer: Roadie's customers include engineering organizations such as Netlify, Snyk, Contentful, and MyFitnessPal.

faqs.question: Does Roadie support AI coding agents
faqs.answer: Yes, Roadie offers RAG-based AI search and MCP server access so AI coding agents can query the organization's software catalog for context.

faqs.question: How is Roadie priced
faqs.answer: Roadie charges per 'contributing developer,' meaning engineers whose code is tracked in the catalog; non-coding users can access the portal for free.

enrichment.short_description: Roadmunk is a product roadmapping tool that helps product teams visualize, prioritize, and communicate strategy across timeline, swimlane, and Kanban roadmap views, now part of Tempo's Strategic Roadmaps suite.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered per-user subscription billed annually, plus a custom Enterprise plan
enrichment.company_size: Part of Tempo (parent company); Roadmunk team historically small (11-50 employees pre-acquisition)
enrichment.headquarters: Toronto, Canada (originally); now operates under Tempo, headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts
enrichment.seo_title: Roadmunk Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Product Roadmap Software Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Roadmunk is a product roadmapping tool, now part of Tempo's Strategic Roadmaps suite. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Roadmunk is a product roadmapping and prioritization tool that helps product managers and cross-functional teams build, visualize, and share roadmaps in multiple formats, including timeline views, swimlane views, and Kanban-style boards, along with prioritization frameworks for scoring and ranking features or initiatives. The company was founded in 2013 and originally headquartered in Toronto, Canada, before being acquired by Tempo, a project and portfolio management software company, in November 2021; since the acquisition, Roadmunk has been rebranded and continues to be sold as 'Strategic Roadmaps (Roadmunk)' within Tempo's broader portfolio of Jira-ecosystem and enterprise agile planning products, with Tempo itself headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts. Roadmunk's core use case is letting product teams collect feedback and requests, score and prioritize items using built-in frameworks, and generate polished, presentation-ready roadmap visuals to share with executives, sales teams, and customers, without needing design or spreadsheet work; it integrates with tools like Jira, Trello, and other product management systems to pull in existing backlog data. Pricing follows a tiered per-user model billed primarily on an annual basis: the Starter plan is priced around $19 per user per month for individuals or very small teams needing basic roadmapping; the Business plan is around $49 per user per month and adds collaboration features suited to small teams, such as feedback management and portfolio-level roadmap views; the Professional plan is around $99 per user per month with more advanced sharing and integration capabilities; and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with additional features like single sign-on (SSO), priority support, and a dedicated customer success manager. All plans include a 14-day free trial. As a tool now embedded within Tempo's Strategic Roadmaps product line, Roadmunk is most relevant to product managers already using or adjacent to the Atlassian/Jira ecosystem, and it competes with dedicated roadmapping tools like ProductPlan, Aha!, and Productboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Roadmunk is a product roadmapping tool that helps product managers visualize, prioritize, and communicate product strategy through timeline, swimlane, and Kanban-style roadmap views. Founded in 2013 and originally based in Toronto, Canada, Roadmunk was acquired by Tempo, a project and portfolio management software company, in November 2021, and now operates as 'Strategic Roadmaps (Roadmunk)' within Tempo's broader suite of tools for Jira-based enterprise planning, with Tempo headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts.

The tool is aimed at product managers and cross-functional teams who need to turn backlog and feedback data into clear, presentation-ready roadmaps for stakeholders, executives, and customers, and it integrates with systems like Jira and Trello to pull in existing planning data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Roadmunk offers multiple roadmap visualization formats, including timeline views for date-driven planning, swimlane views for grouping initiatives by theme or team, and Kanban boards for status-based tracking, all built to be shared externally without additional design work.

The platform also includes prioritization frameworks for scoring and ranking features or initiatives based on criteria like effort and impact, feedback and request collection from stakeholders, and portfolio-level roadmap views for organizations managing multiple products or teams, along with integrations into tools like Jira and Trello.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Roadmunk uses tiered, per-user pricing billed primarily on an annual basis. The Starter plan is priced around $19 per user per month, the Business plan around $49 per user per month, and the Professional plan around $99 per user per month, with each tier adding more collaboration, sharing, and integration capabilities.
An Enterprise plan is available with custom pricing that adds features like single sign-on, priority support, and a dedicated customer success manager, and all plans include a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: How much does Roadmunk cost
faqs.answer: Roadmunk's plans are priced around $19/user/month (Starter), $49/user/month (Business), and $99/user/month (Professional), with a custom Enterprise plan available.

faqs.question: Is Roadmunk still an independent company
faqs.answer: No. Roadmunk was acquired by Tempo in November 2021 and is now sold as 'Strategic Roadmaps (Roadmunk)' within Tempo's product suite.

faqs.answer: Roadmunk was founded in 2013 in Toronto, Canada.

faqs.answer: Yes, Roadmunk integrates with Jira and Trello to pull existing backlog and planning data into roadmap views.

faqs.answer: Yes, all Roadmunk plans include a 14-day free trial.

enrichment.short_description: Robin AI was a London-based AI-powered contract review and legal intelligence platform; the company wound down as an independent business in late 2025, with its managed services team acquired by Scissero and its technology and engineering team absorbed by Microsoft in January 2026.
enrichment.pricing_model: Historically freemium with paid subscription and custom enterprise tiers; no longer independently sold as of 2026
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 100-150 employees at peak (2024-2025); company wound down as an independent business in late 2025/early 2026
enrichment.seo_title: Robin AI 2026 Status: What Happened to the Legal Contract AI Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Robin AI was an AI-powered contract review platform that wound down in late 2025. Learn its history, features, pricing, and what happened to the company.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Robin AI was a London-based, AI-powered contract review and negotiation platform founded in 2019 by Richard Robinson, a former disputes lawyer at Clifford Chance, and James Clough, a machine learning researcher trained at Imperial College London, with headquarters at 10 Devonshire Square in London. The company combined proprietary legal AI models with a human-in-the-loop team of in-house lawyers to help corporate legal and procurement teams review, redline, and negotiate contracts faster, and at its peak reportedly served 13 Fortune 500 companies with around $10 million in annual recurring revenue and a further $16 million sales pipeline. Robin AI raised significant venture funding over its life, including a $26 million round highlighted in a 2024 press release, and was recognized on startup lists such as the UK's Startups 100 in 2025. However, in the second half of 2025 the company's multi-year losses outpaced its revenue growth, and after failing to close a planned $50 million funding round in October 2025, Robin AI laid off roughly one-third of its staff (about 50 roles) and entered a winding-up process and receivership. As a result of this collapse, Robin AI's assets were split and sold in two separate deals in late 2025 and early 2026: its managed services arm, the human-in-the-loop contract review business with around 75 staff, was acquired by the AI-forward law firm Scissero in December 2025, while Microsoft acquired (acqui-hired) Robin AI's technology and engineering team in January 2026 to strengthen legal AI capabilities inside Microsoft Word and its broader productivity suite. Because of this, as of mid-2026 Robin AI no longer operates as an independent, purchasable SaaS product or company; its historical offering combined a freemium tier with paid subscriptions and custom enterprise plans (including API access, advanced integrations, SSO, and SLAs), but exact historical pricing was never fully publicized, and prospective buyers today would instead need to evaluate Scissero (for managed contract review services) or watch for Robin AI's underlying technology resurfacing inside Microsoft's products rather than seeking to purchase 'Robin AI' directly. This makes Robin AI a useful case study in AI-legal-tech startup economics: strong enterprise logos and real revenue were not sufficient to sustain the company through a difficult fundraising environment for applied legal AI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Robin AI was an AI-powered contract review and negotiation platform founded in 2019 by Richard Robinson, a former Clifford Chance disputes lawyer, and James Clough, a machine learning researcher, and headquartered in London, England. The platform combined proprietary legal AI with a human-in-the-loop team of in-house lawyers to help corporate legal and procurement teams review, redline, and negotiate contracts more efficiently, and at its peak served roughly 13 Fortune 500 companies with about $10 million in annual recurring revenue.

In late 2025, however, the company's losses outpaced revenue growth and a planned $50 million funding round fell through in October 2025, leading to layoffs of roughly a third of staff and a formal winding-up process. As a result, Robin AI no longer exists as an independent, purchasable product as of 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Happened to Robin AI
paragraphs: Robin AI's assets were split and sold in two separate transactions. In December 2025, its managed services arm, the human-in-the-loop contract review business employing around 75 staff, was acquired by the AI-forward law firm Scissero. In January 2026, Microsoft separately acqui-hired Robin AI's technology and engineering team to strengthen legal AI capabilities within Microsoft Word and its broader productivity suite.

As a result, businesses that previously used or were evaluating Robin AI as a standalone SaaS platform should look to Scissero for managed contract review services, or monitor how Robin AI's underlying technology may resurface inside Microsoft's products, rather than expecting to purchase or renew a 'Robin AI' subscription directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Historical Pricing
paragraphs: Robin AI historically used a freemium model with a limited free tier, paid subscription plans, and custom enterprise pricing that added API access, advanced integrations, single sign-on, and service-level agreements, but the company never published detailed self-serve pricing, and exact figures are not publicly available.
Because the company wound down as an independent business in late 2025 and early 2026, there is no current, purchasable pricing for Robin AI as a standalone product.

features.title: AI-powered contract review
features.description: Used proprietary legal AI models to review and redline commercial contracts for risk and non-standard clauses.

features.title: Human-in-the-loop legal review
features.description: Combined AI analysis with in-house qualified lawyers who reviewed and validated AI output for accuracy.

features.title: Contract negotiation support
features.description: Assisted legal and procurement teams in negotiating contract terms faster using AI-suggested redlines.

features.title: Enterprise integrations
features.description: Enterprise plans historically included API access and integrations with existing legal and procurement workflows.

features.title: SSO and enterprise security
features.description: Enterprise tier included single sign-on and service-level agreements for larger corporate customers.

features.title: Managed services team
features.description: Roughly 75 staff performed human-in-the-loop contract review as a managed service, now operating under Scissero.

pros: Combined AI speed with qualified human lawyer review for higher accuracy
Served notable enterprise customers including multiple Fortune 500 companies
Founding team combined legal expertise (Clifford Chance) with machine learning research (Imperial College)
Managed services capability continues to operate today under new ownership (Scissero)
cons: Robin AI no longer operates as an independent company or purchasable product as of 2026
Company collapsed after failing to close a $50 million funding round in October 2025
Roughly a third of staff were laid off before the company wound down
Pricing was never fully transparent even while the company was operating
Existing customers had to transition to new providers after the shutdown

faqs.question: Is Robin AI still in business
faqs.answer: No. Robin AI wound down as an independent business in late 2025 and early 2026. Its managed services team was acquired by the law firm Scissero in December 2025, and its technology and engineering team was acqui-hired by Microsoft in January 2026.

faqs.question: What was Robin AI used for
faqs.answer: Robin AI was an AI-powered platform that helped corporate legal and procurement teams review, redline, and negotiate contracts, combining AI analysis with human-in-the-loop lawyer review.

faqs.question: Who founded Robin AI
faqs.answer: Robin AI was founded in 2019 by Richard Robinson, a former Clifford Chance disputes lawyer, and James Clough, a machine learning researcher from Imperial College London.

faqs.question: Why did Robin AI shut down
faqs.answer: Robin AI's multi-year losses outpaced its revenue growth, and after a planned $50 million funding round fell through in October 2025, the company laid off about a third of its staff and entered a winding-up process.

faqs.question: Where can I get contract review services now that Robin AI is gone
faqs.answer: Robin AI's managed services team and human-in-the-loop contract review capability now operate under the law firm Scissero, which acquired that business unit in December 2025.

faqs.question: What happened to Robin AI's technology
faqs.answer: Robin AI's technology and engineering team were acqui-hired by Microsoft in January 2026 to strengthen legal AI features within Microsoft Word and its productivity suite.

faqs.question: How much did Robin AI cost
faqs.answer: Robin AI never published detailed self-serve pricing; it used a freemium model with paid subscriptions and custom enterprise pricing, but exact figures were not publicly disclosed before the company wound down.

faqs.question: Where was Robin AI headquartered
faqs.answer: Robin AI was headquartered in London, England, at 10 Devonshire Square.

use_cases.title: Historical: Enterprise contract review
use_cases.description: Corporate legal teams previously used Robin AI to review high volumes of commercial contracts faster by combining AI redlining with human lawyer verification.
use_cases.audience: In-house legal and procurement teams (historical customers)

use_cases.title: Historical: Contract negotiation acceleration
use_cases.description: Legal teams used Robin AI's AI-suggested redlines to speed up back-and-forth contract negotiation with counterparties.
use_cases.audience: Legal operations and contract managers (historical customers)

use_cases.title: Current: Managed contract review via Scissero
use_cases.description: Organizations seeking human-in-the-loop contract review today can turn to Scissero, which acquired Robin AI's managed services team and business.
use_cases.audience: Legal teams evaluating outsourced contract review providers in 2026
